{{Shrine | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}. The shrine is near [[Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|ja|淀川}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Jōgan era|ja|貞観}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|ja|淀川}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Jōgan era|ja|貞観}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳}}

'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}. The shrine is near [[Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.

Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|ja|淀川}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.

[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.

{{Shrine | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|ja|淀川}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Jōgan era|ja|貞観}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳}}

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|ja|淀川}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Jōgan era|ja|貞観}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|ja|淀川}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Jōgan era|ja|貞観}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.== See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Okami Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Water deities]] [[Category:Gion Shrines]] [[Category:Hiyoshi Shrines]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former village shrines]] [[Category:Haibutsu Kishaku]] [[Category:Sangaku]] [[Category:History of Hirakata]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirakata]] [[Category:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]]<!--Okami Shrine (Hirakata)-->[[Category:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[ja:%E6%84%8F%E8%B3%80%E7%BE%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E6%9E%9A%E6%96%B9%E5%B8%82)]][[Category:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]] <!--Okami Shrine (Hirakata)-->[[Category:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[ja:&action=edit]] [[Category:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]] <!--Okami Shrine (Hirakata)-->[[Category:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]] [[ja:意賀美神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|ja|淀川}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Jōgan era|ja|貞観}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳}}

Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=draft}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額|12=draft}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=draft}}

{{Shrine | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. The shrine is near [[Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=draft}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=draft}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=draft}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=draft}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|ja|淀川|12=draft}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Jōgan era|ja|貞観|12=draft}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=draft}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=draft}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄|12=draft}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|12=draft}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=draft}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=draft}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額|12=draft}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=draft}}

== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山咋神|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=draft}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=draft}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=draft}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=draft}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|ja|淀川|12=draft}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Jōgan era|ja|貞観|12=draft}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=draft}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=draft}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄|12=draft}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|12=draft}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.

Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=draft}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.

Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=draft}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|ja|算額|12=draft}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=draft}}
== References == {{Reflist}} <!--Okami Shrine (Hirakata)--> <!--Okami Shrine (Hirakata)-->
<!--Okami Shrine (Hirakata)-->

[[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Okami Shrine (Hirakata)]] [[ja:%E6%84%8F%E8%B3%80%E7%BE%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E6%9E%9A%E6%96%B9%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit]] [[ja:意賀美神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okami Shrine}}

48 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市) up to rev 99156968
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 意賀美神社 |画像= [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] |所在地= 大阪府枚方市枚方上之町1-12 |位置 = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=it}} |祭神= 高龗神 |社格= 式内社（小）・村社 |本殿= [[流造]] |例祭= [[10月14日]] |地図 = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }} '''意賀美神社'''（おかみじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[枚方市]]にある[[神社]]。[[式内社]]で、旧[[社格]]は[[村社]]。[[枚方市駅]]付近にある。
==祭神== [[淤加美神|高龗神]]を主祭神とし、[[スサノオ|素盞嗚尊]]・[[大山咋神]]・[[大国主|大国主神]]を配祀する。素盞嗚尊は須加神社の、大山咋神・大国主神は日吉神社の祭神で、明治時代に[[神社合祀|合祀]]されたものである<ref name="意賀美神社"/>。
==歴史== [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|河内名所図会「枚方萬年寺」]] 意賀美神社は元は現在地から約100m南の旧伊加賀村字宮山に鎮座していた。創建の年代は不詳であるが、[[開化天皇]]の時代には[[ニギハヤヒ|饒速日命]]の五世の子孫で[[物部氏]]の遠祖である[[伊香色雄|伊香色男]]の邸宅がこの地にあり、その敷地内に鎮座していたという。[[高龗神]]は[[水神]]であり、[[淀川]]の鎮守として創建されたものとみられる。[[延喜式神名帳]]では小社に列している。旧伊加賀村の氏神として、明治5年（[[1872年]]）に村社に列格した。
現鎮座地の万年寺山にあったのは須賀神社であった。須賀神社は元は牛頭天王社（祇園社）と称し、[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]14年（[[872年]]）、流行していた悪疫の終息祈願のため、当地にあった長松山万年寺の開祖・[[聖宝]]が[[牛頭天王]]を勧請して創建された。万年寺は明治3年（[[1870年]]）に[[廃仏毀釈]]により廃寺となった。旧三矢村の氏神として、明治5年（[[1872年]]）に村社に列格した。
日吉神社は岡村の鎮守神で、元は山王大権現と称していた。創建の年代は不詳であるが、一説には淀川の洪水で流れついた[[日吉大社]]の[[神体]]を祀ったのに始まるとも、[[最澄]]（伝教大師）が[[平安京]]の[[鬼門|裏鬼門]]に当たるこの地に阿弥陀仏と日吉大神を祀ったのに始まるとも伝わる。[[応仁の乱]]で焼失したが、[[慶長]]5年（[[1600年]]）に[[枚方城]]主・[[本多内膳正政康]]が再興し、氏神として崇敬した。旧岡村の氏神として、明治5年に村社に列格した。
明治42年（[[1909年]]）10月14日、須加神社と日吉神社は意賀美神社に合祀された。境内が狭いため、翌10月15日、万年寺山の元の須加神社境内に遷座した。同年12月、神饌幣帛料供進社に指定された。昭和9年（[[1934年]]）、大風害により社殿・社務所が大破し、翌昭和10年（[[1935年]]）に再建された<ref name="意賀美神社">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=意賀美神社 |publisher=大阪府神社庁 |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>。
==境内== 境内社として稲荷社・琴平社がある。
また旧万年寺のものとして、当社には石造層塔のみが残されている。浄念寺には十一面観音像、薬師像、役行者像、鐘楼が残されている<ref name="枚方市の社寺建築">{{Cite book |和書 |title=枚方市建造物調査報告III 枚方市の社寺建築 |publisher=枚方市教育委員会 |date=1994-03-31 |pages=67-71 |chapter=意賀美神社}}</ref>。
[[画像:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|枚方八景「万年寺山の緑陰」]] [[画像:Ogami bairin 20070101.jpg|thumb|意賀美梅林]] 「万年寺山の緑陰」が[[枚方八景]]の一つに選ばれている。境内には約100本の梅の木が植えられており、梅の名所となっている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=京阪沿線 梅の名所 意賀美神社 |publisher=京阪電車 |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>。
* 稲荷社 - 一間社流造 板葺、[[18世紀]]後半<ref name="枚方市の社寺建築" /> * 琴平社 - 一間社流造 銅板葺、[[享保]]5年（[[1720年]]）ごろ。旧須賀神社本殿と伝わるが、信憑性には疑問が残る<ref name="枚方市の社寺建築" /> * 石造層塔 - 石造九重塔 [[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]] - [[室町時代]]初期。もとは十三重塔であったとみられている<ref name="枚方市の社寺建築" />
<gallery mode=packed> ファイル:意賀美神社06駐車場から北方向.jpg|意賀美神社駐車場から北方向の景色 ファイル:意賀美神社01.jpg|意賀美神社鳥居 ファイル:意賀美神社 (枚方市) Z6A 3542 C01.jpg|参道（東側） ファイル:意賀美神社02.jpg|意賀美神社参道（西側） ファイル:意賀美神社 (枚方市) Z6A 3517 C01.jpg|参道（西側） ファイル:意賀美神社03.jpg|意賀美神社参道入口付近 ファイル:意賀美神社04.jpg|意賀美神社付近道標 ファイル:意賀美神社05駐車場.jpg|意賀美神社駐車場 ファイル:意賀美神社 (枚方市) Z6A 3530 C01.jpg|境内 ファイル:意賀美神社 (枚方市) Z6A 3529 C01.jpg|境内 </gallery>
==文化財== *[[算額]] 文久元年 岩田清庸奉納<ref name="意賀美神社"/><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=枚方市内の市指定文化財-意賀美神社の算額（有形民俗文化財） |publisher=枚方市 |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== *[[万年山古墳]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おかみしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:水神]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:日吉神社]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:神仏分離]] [[Category:算額]] [[Category:枚方市の歴史]] [[category:枚方市の建築物]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. The shrine is near [[Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamatsumi]], and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodo River]] and is listed as a minor shrine in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan era]]. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|qq=}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] divine body or a shrine established by [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]]. After being destroyed during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社}}: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] to early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|qq=}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * [[:en:Sangaku|Sangaku]]: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|qq=}}

The shrine is near [[Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamatsumi]], and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.

[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|qq=}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.

It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. The shrine is near [[Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamatsumi]], and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodo River]] and is listed as a minor shrine in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan era]]. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|qq=}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] divine body or a shrine established by [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]]. After being destroyed during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社}}: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] to early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|qq=}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * [[:en:Sangaku|Sangaku]]: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Shrine | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamatsumi]], and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodo River]] and is listed as a minor shrine in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan era]]. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|qq=}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] divine body or a shrine established by [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]]. After being destroyed during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社}}: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] to early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|qq=}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * [[:en:Sangaku|Sangaku]]: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Shrine | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamatsumi]], and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodo River]] and is listed as a minor shrine in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan era]]. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|qq=}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] divine body or a shrine established by [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]]. After being destroyed during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社}}: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] to early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|qq=}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * [[:en:Sangaku|Sangaku]]: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|qq=}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * [[:en:Sangaku|Sangaku]]: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|qq=}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "paes with no wikidata" to "Pages with no Wikidata"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Shrine | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|大阪府]], Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|枚方市駅]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamatsumi]], and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodo River]] and is listed as a minor shrine in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan era]]. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|qq=}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] divine body or a shrine established by [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]]. After being destroyed during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社}}: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] to early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|qq=}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * [[:en:Sangaku|Sangaku]]: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|qq=}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
