== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamatsumi]], and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the [[:en:Yodo River|Yodo River]] and is listed as a minor shrine in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan era]]. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] divine body or a shrine established by [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]]. After being destroyed during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]], it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]], around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] to early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Eight Views of Hirakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * [[:en:Sangaku|Sangaku]]: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannenyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}

'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=大阪府}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|Hirakata-shi Station]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamatsumi}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan era}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Eight Views of Hirakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannenyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}

Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Eight Views of Hirakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannenyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11493038]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:23, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=大阪府}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|Hirakata-shi Station]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamatsumi}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan era}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Eight Views of Hirakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannenyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}}. Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Eight Views of Hirakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.

It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|Hirakata-shi Station]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamatsumi}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan era}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Eight Views of Hirakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannenyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}

It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|Hirakata-shi Station]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamatsumi}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan era}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Eight Views of Hirakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannenyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}

After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Eight Views of Hirakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannenyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] { { {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}} | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=大阪府}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|Hirakata-shi Station]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan era}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Eight Views of Hirakata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannenyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. [[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Q11533401|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] { { {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=大阪府|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|Hirakata-shi Station]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan era|1=Zhenguan}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō|1=Saichō}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Q11533401|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku|1=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannen-yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Q11533401|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.

These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan era|1=Zhenguan}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō|1=Saichō}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Q11533401|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku|1=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannen-yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}

'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=大阪府|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|Hirakata-shi Station]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.

Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}. The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan era|1=Zhenguan}}. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō|1=Saichō}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Q11533401|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.

In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Q11533401|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku|1=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannen-yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Okami-jinja]][[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:意賀美神社 (枚方市)]] { { {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okami Shrine (意賀美神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okami-jinja.jpg|300px|]] | location = 1-12 Hirakata Kamino-cho, Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34|48|48.7|N|135|38|37.5|E|region:JP-27_scale:5000|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) | Shrine_Rank = Shikinaisha (Minor), Murasha | Main_building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | Festival = October 14 | Map = {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=220}} }}
'''Okami Shrine''' (意賀美神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Hirakata City|枚方市]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=大阪府|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was classified as a {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. The shrine is near [[:en:Hirakata-shi Station|Hirakata-shi Station]].
== Deities == The shrine enshrines Takeminakata-no-Kami (高龗神) as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as auxiliary deities. Susanoo was originally worshipped at Suga Shrine, while Ōyamatsumi and Ōkuninushi were enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine. These were merged into Okami Shrine during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}<ref name="OkamiShrine">{{Cite web|url=http://osakadai3shibu.kilo.jp/jinja/hirakata/jinja/okami.html |title=Okami Shrine |publisher=Osaka Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>.
== History == [[File:河内名所図会 枚方萬年寺.png|thumb|Illustration of Mannenji from {{ill|Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja|河内名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] Originally located approximately 100 meters south of its current site in the former Ikaga Village, the shrine's exact foundation date is unknown. It is said to have been established on the estate of {{ill|Ika-shikoo|ja|伊香色男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ika-shikoo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}} and a descendant of {{ill|Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}. Takeminakata-no-Kami, a water deity, was worshipped as the guardian of the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}} and is listed as a minor shrine in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}. It became the tutelary shrine of Ikaga Village and was designated a village shrine in 1872 (Meiji 5).
The current location was previously occupied by Suga Shrine, originally called Gozu Tenno Shrine (Gion Shrine), established in 872 CE during the {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|WD=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan era|1=Zhenguan}}. The shrine was founded to pray for the end of a plague by {{ill|Shōbō|ja|聖宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōbō|1=Shōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610974}}, the founder of {{ill|Mannenji Temple|ja|長松山万年寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was abolished during the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} movement in 1870. Suga Shrine also became a village shrine in 1872.
Hiyoshi Shrine, previously known as Sannō Daigongen, was the guardian deity of the nearby Okamura area. Its origins are unclear but may trace back to either a drifted {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} divine body or a shrine established by {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|WD=Q449943|lt=Saichō|1=Saichō}}. After being destroyed during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, it was rebuilt in 1600 by Matsudaira Naizen-no-kami Masayasu, lord of Hirakata Castle.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), Suga Shrine and Hiyoshi Shrine were merged into Okami Shrine. Due to space limitations, the shrine was relocated the following day to its current site on Mannenji Hill, the former location of Suga Shrine<ref name="OkamiShrine"/>.
== Grounds == The shrine grounds include the following: * {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, late 18th century<ref name="HirakataShrines">{{Cite book|title=Hirakata City Report III: Shrine Architecture in Hirakata|publisher=Hirakata City Board of Education|date=1994|pages=67-71}}</ref>. * Kotohira Shrine: Built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, around 1720 (Kyōhō 5). Believed to have been the former main hall of Suga Shrine, though this is uncertain<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>. * Stone Pagoda: A nine-tiered pagoda dating back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} to early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. Originally thought to be a thirteen-tiered structure<ref name="HirakataShrines"/>.
[[File:Hirakata hakkei Mannenjiyama no ryokuin 20070101.jpg|thumb|Mannenji Hill, one of the {{ill|Eight Views of Hirakata|ja|枚方八景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Views of Hirakata|1=Q11533401|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11533401}}]] The site is renowned for its approximately 100 plum trees, making it a popular plum-viewing spot<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.okeihan.net/recommend/ume/detail/05_okami.php |title=Plum Viewing at Okami Shrine |publisher=Keihan Railway |accessdate=2020-07-17 }}</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * {{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku|1=Sangaku}}: Dedicated in 1861 (Bunkyū 1) by Iwata Seiyō<ref name="OkamiShrine"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.hirakata.osaka.jp/0000002755.html#link-si |title=Hirakata City Cultural Properties |publisher=Hirakata City |accessdate=2020-07-18 }}</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|Mannenyama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannenyama Kofun|1=Mannen-yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353824}}
